title: Filesystem Hierarchy Standard
description: The Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) defines the directory structure on Linux systems. Knowing where things live — and why — is table-stakes for every Production infrastructure interview. When an interviewer says 'your service is logging to /var/log and filling up the disk,' you need to know where everything is.

sections.title: The Root Filesystem (/)
sections.description: Everything on Linux starts at /. The root filesystem is the top of the single unified directory tree — there are no drive letters (C:, D:). Every storage device is mounted at a directory under /. This unified namespace is one of Unix's most important innovations.
sections.codeBlock:
# Essential root directories at a glance
/bin      # Essential user command binaries (available in single-user mode)
/sbin     # System administration binaries (e.g., fdisk, mkfs, iptables)
/etc      # Host-specific system configuration (the nerve center)
/var      # Variable data — logs, databases, spool files
/tmp      # Temporary files (cleared on boot, world-writable)
/usr      # Secondary hierarchy — user utilities and applications
/opt      # Add-on application software packages
/proc     # Virtual filesystem for process and kernel information
/sys      # Virtual filesystem for kernel objects and device info
/dev      # Device files (every hardware device is a file)
/run      # Runtime variable data (PID files, sockets — tmpfs since boot)
/home     # User home directories
/root     # Root user's home directory

# A Quick map:
# /etc  → Configuration
# /var  → Data (logs, DBs)
# /proc → Kernel/process introspection
# /dev  → Hardware as files
# /tmp  → Scratch space (watch for security!)

sections.title: /etc — The Configuration Nerve Center
sections.description: /etc holds all host-specific configuration. If something isn't working, you start here. In Production environments, /etc is typically managed by configuration management (Ansible, Puppet, Chef) and changes are reviewed through code review.
sections.codeBlock:
ls /etc
# Key files every engineer should know:
/etc/passwd       # User account info (login shells, UIDs)
/etc/shadow       # Encrypted password hashes (root-readable only)
/etc/group        # Group definitions
/etc/sudoers      # Sudo privileges (edit with visudo!)
/etc/hosts        # Static hostname-to-IP mapping
/etc/resolv.conf  # DNS resolver configuration
/etc/hostname     # System hostname
/etc/fstab        # Filesystem mount table (auto-mount on boot)
/etc/crontab      # System-wide cron schedule
/etc/ssh/sshd_config   # SSH server configuration
/etc/nginx/nginx.conf  # Nginx configuration (if installed)
/etc/systemd/    # Systemd unit files and system configuration

# Tip: /etc is typically on the root filesystem
# Don't let logs fill up / — it can make the system unbootable

sections.title: /proc & /sys — Virtual Filesystems for Kernel Introspection
sections.description: /proc and /sys are virtual filesystems — they don't exist on disk. Reading files in /proc triggers kernel code that returns real-time data. Writing to some files changes kernel parameters. These are your window into the running kernel. Every Production SRE knows /proc intimately.
sections.codeBlock:
# === /proc — Process and System Information ===
cat /proc/cpuinfo      # CPU details (model, cores, flags, cache)
cat /proc/meminfo      # Memory: total, free, buffers, cache, swap
cat /proc/uptime       # System uptime in seconds
cat /proc/version      # Linux kernel version string
cat /proc/loadavg      # System load averages (1, 5, 15 min)
cat /proc/1/cmdline    # Command line of PID 1 (init/systemd)
cat /proc/self/status  # Current process's status (Name, Pid, VmRSS, ...)
ls /proc/<PID>/fd/     # Open file descriptors for a process
cat /proc/<PID>/maps   # Memory-mapped regions of a process

# === /proc/sys — Kernel Tunables (sysctl interface) ===
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_forward        # Is IP forwarding enabled?
cat /proc/sys/net/ipv4/tcp_tw_reuse      # TCP TIME_WAIT reuse
cat /proc/sys/vm/swappiness              # Swap tendency (0-100)
cat /proc/sys/vm/dirty_ratio             # Max dirty pages %% before writeback

# === /sys — Device and Driver Model ===
ls /sys/class/block/    # All block devices (sda, nvme0n1, etc.)
ls /sys/class/net/      # All network interfaces (eth0, lo, wlan0)
ls /sys/devices/system/cpu/  # Per-CPU information

# Enterprise insight: When debugging production issues,
# /proc/<PID>/ is your first stop for process-level diagnostics

sections.title: /var — Variable Data (Logs, Databases, Spool)
sections.description: /var holds data that changes in size and content during normal operation. This is where logs go, and disk space monitoring of /var is a standard part of any SRE runbook. Common Interview scenario: 'Disk is full, find and clean up /var/log without restarting services.'
sections.codeBlock:
ls /var
/var/log          # System and application logs (journalctl, syslog, nginx)
/var/lib          # Persistent state data (databases: mysql, postgres, docker)
/var/cache        # Application cache data (apt, yum, pip)
/var/spool        # Print and mail spools
/var/tmp          # Temporary files preserved across reboots
/var/opt          # Variable data for /opt packages

# === Production scenario: Log Rotation ===
# Logrotate manages log rotation. Config at /etc/logrotate.conf
# Watch for: services that log too verbosely, filling /var

# Check disk usage by directory:
du -sh /var/log/* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -10

# Safely truncate a log without restarting:
# (The file descriptor stays valid even after truncation)
> /var/log/myapp/access.log          # Truncate in place
# OR:
truncate -s 0 /var/log/myapp/access.log

sections.title: Interview Q&A
sections.description:
Q: 'Your production server is running out of disk space on /. Walk through your debugging process.'

A: 'First, df -h to see which filesystem is full. Then du -sh /* | sort -rh to find the largest top-level directory. Common culprits: /var/log (unrotated logs), /tmp (temp files from batch jobs), Docker overlay storage in /var/lib/docker. For /var/log, check logrotate config. For Docker, prune unused images and containers. In a Enterprise environment, we'd have monitoring alerting on disk usage at 80%, 90%, 95% — and automated cleanup via cron or systemd timers.'

Q: 'What's the difference between /proc and /sys?'

A: '/proc was originally process information — the original design was one directory per process (/proc/PID). It evolved to include system info (/proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo, /proc/sys). /sys (sysfs) is newer — it exports the kernel's device and driver model in a structured way. /sys/class organizes by type (block, net, input), /sys/devices by topology. Both are virtual filesystems backed by kernel callbacks. Use /proc for process data and kernel tunables; use /sys for hardware and driver information.'

Q: 'Why does Linux use forward slashes (/) instead of backslashes (\\)?'

A: 'Historical compatibility with the MULTICS/Unix design choice. Forward slashes were chosen because they didn't conflict with the shell's escape character (backslash). Every major OS except Windows uses forward slashes.'

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Filesystem Hierarchy Standard 3.0 — The official FHS specification
  • man hier — Description of the filesystem hierarchy on your system
  • Linux Filesystem Hierarchy — TLDP comprehensive guide

learningObjectives: Understand /proc & /sys — Virtual Filesystems for Kernel Introspection

analogy.title: Filesystem as a Library
analogy.description: The root directory (/) is the main entrance. /bin and /sbin are the reference section (essential tools). /etc is the catalog system (configuration). /var is the archives where new records (logs) arrive daily. /home is personal study carrels. /proc and /sys are live feeds showing what the library is doing right now.

commonMistakes.mistake: Ignoring log rotation until disk fills up
commonMistakes.solution: Configure logrotate before deploying; monitor /var/log usage

commonMistakes.mistake: Disabling SELinux/AppArmor instead of fixing policies
commonMistakes.solution: Use `audit2allow` to generate policies from denials; keep MAC enabled
Add `USER nonroot` in Dockerfile; use `--user` flag at runtime

commonMistakes.mistake: Not setting resource limits on containers
commonMistakes.solution: Always set `--cpus` and `--memory`; configure cgroup limits in Kubernetes

practiceLabs.title: Explore: The Root Filesystem (/)
practiceLabs.task: Run `ls` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man ls` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: /etc — The Configuration Nerve Center
practiceLabs.task: Run `cat` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man cat` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

practiceLabs.title: Explore: /proc & /sys — Virtual Filesystems for Kernel Introspection
practiceLabs.task: Run `du` and examine the output. Note the structure, key fields, and patterns you observe. Try different flags to see how the output changes.
practiceLabs.expectedOutput: Output will vary by system. Look for structured fields, key identifiers, and configuration sections.
practiceLabs.hint: Use `man du` to see all available flags and understand the output columns.

quickReference.title: Quick Reference
quickReference.content:
| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `ls` | /etc — The Configuration Nerve Center |
| `cat` | /proc & /sys — Virtual Filesystems for Kernel Introspection |
| `ls` | /proc & /sys — Virtual Filesystems for Kernel Introspection |
| `ls` | /var — Variable Data (Logs, Databases, Spool) |
| `du` | /var — Variable Data (Logs, Databases, Spool) |
| `truncate` | /var — Variable Data (Logs, Databases, Spool) |

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect arguments, wrong working directory, or stale cached data
troubleshooting.solution: Verify arguments with `man ls`; check current path with `pwd`; clear cache if applicable

troubleshooting.cause: Service not reloaded or syntax error in config file
troubleshooting.solution: Validate config syntax with built-in test flag; reload service with `systemctl reload` or `restart`

troubleshooting.symptom: filesystem operation slow or timing out
troubleshooting.cause: Resource exhaustion (disk I/O, memory pressure, or lock contention)
troubleshooting.solution: Check `iostat`, `free -m`, and `top`; look for stuck processes with `ps aux --sort=-%cpu`

troubleshooting.cause: Incorrect file ownership or overly restrictive permissions
troubleshooting.solution: Inspect with `ls -la`; fix with `chown`/ `chmod`; check ACLs with `getfacl`

quiz.question: What is The Root Filesystem (/)?
options: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
A tool for managing disk partitions
quiz.answer: A standardized directory structure that defines where files and directories should be placed on Linux systems
quiz.explanation: The The Root Filesystem (/) is the foundation of the Linux filesystem — everything in the system is organized under this structure.

quiz.question: What does `ls` do?
quiz.answer: Lists files and directories in the current or specified path
quiz.explanation: Use `ls` to explore directory contents. Combine with flags like `-l` (long format), `-a` (hidden files), and `-h` (human-readable sizes).

quiz.question: Which command would you use to view disk usage of the filesystem directory?
quiz.explanation: `du -sh` (disk usage, summary, human-readable) shows the total size of a directory and its contents.

quiz.question: A user reports "Permission denied" when trying to access a filesystem directory. What is the first command you should run?
quiz.explanation: `ls -ld` shows the directory's permissions and ownership. Always inspect before making changes — starting with `chmod 777` is a security anti-pattern.

quiz.question: In a filesystem scenario, your monitoring alerts show ls failing intermittently. What is the correct debugging workflow?
options: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
Ignore the alert and wait for it to resolve
quiz.answer: Check logs → verify configuration → test in staging → apply fix → monitor
quiz.explanation: A structured debugging workflow prevents cascading failures. Always diagnose before acting, especially in production environments where downtime has direct business impact.

interviewTips: FAANG Architect's Note:  The Unix filesystem hierarchy isn't arbitrary — it reflects the system's engineering philosophy:  everything is a file
productionTips: Production Tip:  In containerized environments (Docker/Kubernetes), some of these directories behave differently
bestPractices: Use version control for all configuration (/etc, scripts, Dockerfiles)
Implement infrastructure as code; avoid manual server changes
Test changes in staging before production; have rollback plans
enterprisePerspective: In enterprise environments, filesystem hierarchy standard is managed through automation (Ansible, Terraform), monitored centrally (Prometheus, Datadog), and governed by compliance policies (CIS benchmarks, SOC2). Changes go through change advisory boards with rollback procedures documented.
